title: Comparison of Protocols in  Embryo-Cultured Macapuno Coconut
author: Martinez-Tagbo, Rose Ann
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Macapuno, the very soft endosperm of a coconut, is a commercially important crop. It is rare and high-value because the endosperm enclosing normal embryos impedes germination, effectively making the seeds sterile. Embryo - Culture of Macapuno (ECM) is the most efficient method of germinating normal embryos in vitro enclosed by the jelly-like solid endosperm of macapuno. In this study, two ECM protocols were compared to assess which produce viable plantlets that could be field-planted and produced high-yielding coconut seedlings. In Protocol 1, the explants received 7% sucrose in liquid media for 2 months, 5 months in solid media, and then back to liquid media until they are transplanted in soil medium or removed after 12 months of not developing. In Protocol 2, explants were germinated in liquid medium in the first month, solid media for growing explants with shoot and root primordia in the second month, and solid again for the remainder of the third and fourth months. In the fifth month, plantlets were supplied with 8% sucrose, while the remaining plants were grown in liquid media containing 8% sucrose. Results showed that Protocol 1 produced 11.33% plantlets in vitro, 2.67% were transplanted to soil media, and there was no survival during acclimatization stage while Protocol 2 have 25% plantlets in vitro, 16% were transplanted to soil media and there were 2.5% existing plantlets in vitro and 2.5% plantlets in acclimatization stage. Solid medium substantially aids in the induction of shoots and roots in the early stages of explants. Increasing sucrose levels from 7 to 8% when the stem of explants transforms into leaflets and soaking of seedlings to Indole-3-butyric acid (IBA) solution also benefits plant development. Efforts are still needed to adjust the protocols and adapt the explants for optimal germination, shooting, and rooting to improve the yields of coconut seedlings.
